Brief Description: Restricts ALPR use to law enforcement officers only; data retention limited to 3 minutes unless connected to active investigation.
Requires all ALPR operators (public and private) to post a usage and privacy policy online; CHP data retention limited to 60 days.
Limits ALPR data retention to 18 months; requires annual reporting to legislature on ALPR usage.
Limits ALPR data retention to 21 days unless part of active criminal investigation.
Restricts ALPR data use to criminal justice purposes; limits retention; prohibits sale of data; requires audits.
Requires passive surveillance records (including ALPR data) to be destroyed within 3 years; access restricted after 1 year.
Classifies ALPR data as private; restricts use; requires law enforcement policies for ALPR use.
Restricts ALPR use to governmental entities for criminal justice purposes; limits data retention; requires annual reporting.
Limits ALPR data retention; restricts use to law enforcement purposes; requires warrant for historical queries.
Requires ALPR data retention limits of 90 days; mandates audits and reporting of system use.
First statewide ALPR regulation in Virginia; defines authorized users; 21-day data retention; restricts data sharing.
Defines authorized ALPR use for law enforcement and traffic flow; requires training; prohibits non-law enforcement purposes.
Limits ALPR data retention to 30 months; restricts sharing to criminal justice agencies; prohibits sale.
Authorizes law enforcement ALPR use; restricts data sharing; 2025 amendment expanded access to private entities.
First U.S. city to ban government use of facial recognition technology, including by police.
Implementing Agency: SF Board of Supervisors / Committee on Information Technology

Brief Description: Second U.S. city to ban municipal use of facial recognition; includes biometric surveillance.
Bans city acquisition and use of facial recognition technology as amendment to existing surveillance ordinance.
Implementing Agency: City of Oakland Privacy Advisory Commission

Brief Description: Bans city use of facial recognition and prohibits police from requesting other agencies use it on their behalf.
Bans both government and private-sector use of facial recognition in places of public accommodation; broadest U.S. ban.
Bans city departments from procuring or using facial recognition technology; bans use of data obtained from it.
Bans city use of facial recognition and predictive policing technology. Amended in 2022 to allow limited use for violent crimes.
Bans city use of facial recognition technology by city departments.
Bans municipal use of facial recognition technology and predictive policing tools.
Bans county use of facial recognition with narrow exceptions for redacting recordings; requires council approval for surveillance tech.
Banned local law enforcement from using facial recognition without legislative approval. Amended in 2022 to allow limited use.
Limits law enforcement use of facial recognition to serious crimes; requires notice; prohibits sole-basis arrests.
Requires warrant for law enforcement facial recognition use; notice to defendants; limits to serious crimes.
Requires government agencies to file notice and test for accuracy and bias before deploying facial recognition; prohibits sole-basis arrests.
Requires city council approval before any city department acquires surveillance technology; mandates impact assessments.
Requires city council approval and community impact reports before acquiring surveillance equipment.
Requires Metro Council approval before police or other agencies acquire surveillance technologies.
Requires city council authorization for surveillance technology acquisition; annual reporting.
Requires board of supervisors approval for surveillance tech; bans facial recognition; mandates annual reports.
Requires city council approval before acquiring surveillance technology; first small city to adopt CCOPS model.

Policy Name / Number: Transparency and Responsible Use of Surveillance Technology (TRUST)
Brief Description: Requires public review and board of supervisors approval before county agencies acquire surveillance technology.
Implementing Agency: San Diego County Board of Supervisors

Brief Description: Three-year moratorium on use of facial recognition and biometric surveillance in police body cameras statewide.
First comprehensive U.S. state consumer privacy law; grants rights to access, delete, and opt out of sale of personal data.
Implementing Agency: California AG / California Privacy Protection Agency

Brief Description: Ballot initiative amending and expanding CCPA; created California Privacy Protection Agency; added data minimization and correction rights.
Second state comprehensive privacy law; grants consumer rights; became model for many subsequent state laws.
Third state comprehensive privacy law; applies to nonprofits; 2024 amendment added neural data as sensitive data category.
Fifth state comprehensive privacy law; later amended (SB 3, 2023) to add children's data protections.
Fourth state comprehensive privacy law; business-friendly approach with fewer consumer rights than California.
Sixth state comprehensive privacy law; narrower consumer rights; no right to correct data.
Modeled on Virginia/Colorado framework; effective January 1, 2026.
Tennessee Information Protection Act; modeled on Virginia framework.
Comprehensive privacy law for second-largest state; no revenue threshold; small business exemption.
Low population threshold (50,000 consumers); includes nonprofits.
Among strongest state privacy laws; applies to nonprofits; includes biometric and sensitive data protections.
Applies to more businesses than most state laws; opt-in consent for minors under 17 (highest threshold).
Lower applicability thresholds (35,000 consumers); modeled on early Connecticut law.
Comprehensive privacy law with opt-in consent for sensitive data processing.
Modeled on Virginia framework; effective January 1, 2026.
Among strongest state privacy laws; strong data minimization; applies to nonprofits.
Comprehensive privacy law with consumer rights to access, correct, delete, and port data.
Modeled on Texas framework.
Twentieth state comprehensive privacy law; no cure period for violations.
Biometric Information Privacy Act; private right of action for biometric data violations; generated billions in litigation.
Broad health data privacy law covering consumer health data beyond HIPAA; private right of action.
Requires data brokers to register; creates one-click mechanism for consumers to request deletion from all registered brokers.
First state to require data brokers to register annually with the Secretary of State.
Codified ABC test for worker classification; presumption of employee status. Triggered national debate on gig worker rights.
Ballot initiative ($200M+ campaign) exempting app-based drivers from AB5; provides minimum earnings and benefits.
Establishes framework for TNC (ride-hail) drivers; minimum per-mile/per-minute pay; paid sick leave; workers' comp.
Established minimum per-trip compensation for Uber/Lyft drivers; first U.S. city to set rideshare driver minimum wage.
Minimum payment standards for app-based delivery workers; paid sick time; transparency requirements. Effective Jan 2024.
Established minimum pay rate for app-based food delivery workers ($17.96/hr, rising to $19.96 by April 2025).
Implementing Agency: NYC Dept. of Consumer and Worker Protection

Brief Description: Suite of bills granting food delivery workers rights: restroom access, trip transparency, maximum distance settings.
AG sued Uber/Lyft for misclassification (2020); industry-backed ballot initiative to create contractor-plus category withdrew (2024).
Established minimum pay per ride for Uber/Lyft drivers; triggered threats from companies to leave the market.
Established task force on gig economy worker classification; studied portable benefits models.
Requires STR hosts to register with city; must be primary residence; host must be present; platforms must verify registration. Reduced Airbnb listings by 90%+.
Legalized and regulated STRs; primary residence only; 90-day cap for unhosted rentals; mandatory registration.
Effectively banned whole-home vacation rentals; only home-sharing (host present) allowed; wiped out ~80% of Airbnb listings.
Banned new non-owner-occupied STR permits in residential zones; phased out existing ones by 2020.
Prohibited new non-owner-occupied STR permits in residential zones; all Type-2 permits expired by 2022.
Limits STRs to primary residence; 120-day annual cap; registration and platform accountability requirements.
Registration requirement; prohibits single-night rentals; three-strikes rule for nuisance violations.
Raised minimum rental period to 90 days in most residential areas; effectively banned most STRs.
First statewide STR framework in U.S.; requires platform tax collection/remittance and quarterly reporting.
Implementing Agency: NY Dept. of Taxation and Finance

Brief Description: Announced phase-out of all 10,000+ tourist apartment licenses by November 2028.
Banned new short-term rental licenses in UNESCO-listed historic city center.
First U.S. state to authorize operation of autonomous vehicles; established testing framework.
Authorized DMV to develop regulations for testing and deployment of autonomous vehicles on public roads.
Governor Ducey issued executive order enabling AV testing without prior permits; made Arizona most permissive state.
Authorized operation of AVs without conventional driver; defined vehicle owner as 'operator' for liability purposes.
Authorized fully autonomous vehicles to operate on Florida roads without human driver present.
Requires manufacturers to continuously monitor every AV on road; designate remote human operator; allows local enforcement of traffic laws.
Board of Supervisors passed resolution opposing CPUC approval of expanded Waymo/Cruise commercial robotaxi service.
Authorized testing of highly automated vehicles with human safety driver; established PennDOT oversight.
Authorized AVs without human driver; established insurance requirements.
Comprehensive AV legislation allowing driverless testing and deployment; established regulatory framework.
City-owned electric utility expanded 10 Gbps fiber network; became national model for municipal broadband.
Removed barriers to municipal broadband; authorized local governments to provide broadband service.
Authorized port districts and public utility districts to provide broadband services directly to consumers.
Relaxed restrictions on municipal broadband that had been in place since 2011; allowed electric cooperatives to offer broadband.
Implementing Agency: NC Dept. of IT / Broadband Office

Brief Description: Created independent state authority to coordinate broadband expansion and manage federal broadband funds.
Created state broadband office and advisory council; authorized $700M+ in state broadband funding.
Implementing Agency: Virginia Dept. of Housing and Community Development

Brief Description: Voters approved city to build and operate municipal broadband network after overcoming state preemption law.
Largest state broadband investment; created open-access middle-mile network; funded last-mile grants.
Required ISPs to offer $15/month broadband plans to qualifying low-income households. Blocked by court as preempted.
First U.S. law requiring bias audits for AI hiring tools; mandates notice to candidates; enforced from July 2023.
Implementing Agency: NYC Dept. of Consumer and Worker Protection

Brief Description: First U.S. law regulating AI in hiring; requires consent for AI-analyzed video interviews; right to deletion.
Expanded AI hiring regulation beyond video interviews; prohibits AI that discriminates based on protected class.
First state comprehensive AI law; requires developers and deployers of high-risk AI to use reasonable care to avoid algorithmic discrimination.
Banned use of predictive policing technology by city departments.
Banned use of predictive policing technology alongside facial recognition ban.
LAPD discontinued use of PredPol predictive policing software following public pressure and audits showing racial bias.
Requires businesses using generative AI to disclose AI interaction to consumers.
Implementing Agency: Utah Dept. of Commerce AI Policy Office

Brief Description: First U.S. AADC modeled on UK version; requires DPIAs for services likely accessed by minors; default high privacy settings.
Requires age verification and parental consent for minors on social media; curfew (10:30pm-6:30am); limits messaging.
Requires social media platforms (>$100M revenue) to verify age of new users; parental consent for under-18.
Requires digital service providers to get parental consent before minors under 18 create accounts.
Requires platforms to terminate accounts of users under 14; parental consent for ages 14-15.

Policy Name / Number: SB 351 (Protecting Georgia's Children on Social Media)
Brief Description: Requires age verification and parental consent for minors on social media; restricts school social media use.

Policy Name / Number: S.7694/A.8148 (SAFE for Kids Act) and S.7695/A.8149 (NY CHILD Act)
Brief Description: Requires parental consent before providing addictive algorithmic feeds to minors; restricts notification timing.

Policy Name / Number: SB 976 (Protecting Kids from Social Media Addiction)
Brief Description: Prohibits platforms from providing addictive feeds to minors without parental consent; restricts late-night notifications.
AADC-style law requiring high default privacy settings for under-16 users; DPIAs for children's data processing.
Requires social media platforms to verify age, obtain parental consent for minors, limit targeted advertising.
First state to require age verification for websites with content harmful to minors (primarily pornographic).
Requires platforms to verify age; limits minors to one hour per day per app without parental consent.
Requires compensation and record-keeping for minors creating online content; modeled on child labor protections.
Implementing Agency: Minnesota Dept. of Labor and Industry

Brief Description: Restricted data center construction to certain zones; imposed noise limits; required setbacks from residential areas.
Updated zoning for data centers in 'Data Center Alley'; imposed design standards and noise requirements.
Imposed temporary moratorium on new data center applications; updated comprehensive plan with siting standards.
Rolled back 2021 laws that had preempted municipal noise ordinances for data centers and crypto mines.
Modified sales tax exemption requirements for data centers; added job creation thresholds.
Longstanding sales and use tax exemption for data center equipment; one of the nation's most generous programs.
Updated zoning to address data center water consumption; imposed water recycling requirements.
Revised enterprise zone tax incentives for data centers; added workforce and community benefit requirements.
Created overlay district with specific siting, noise, and screening requirements for data centers.
Created tax incentive program for data centers meeting investment thresholds; sales tax exemptions.
Placed effective moratorium on new data center grid connections in Dublin area due to electricity capacity concerns.
Implementing Agency: EirGrid / Commission for Regulation of Utilities

Brief Description: Imposed moratorium on new data center construction due to energy and land constraints; selectively lifted in 2022.
Imposed temporary halt on new data center construction in Amsterdam metropolitan area over energy and space concerns.
